retrieved_docs.title: Hearing Loss in the Elderly -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: To best examine, diagnose, and treat hearing loss, standard definitions and agreements are required among health care practitioners. Understanding hearing loss through anatomy, pathophysiology, and epidemiology is key to adequately addressing this condition that is rising with the aging population.

retrieved_docs.title: Newborn Hearing Screening -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hearing tests, as a part of the non-biochemical newborn screening, can identify congenital hearing loss, although infants or children can acquire progressive hearing loss or deafness after birth for various reasons. Based on the etiology, hearing loss divides into sensorineural, conductive, or mixed type. Sensorineural hearing loss is the most common form of hearing loss in the neonates, with 50% of children have the genetic cause. Meanwhile, acquired hearing loss is commonly the result of infectious diseases, especially meningitis, the trauma of the nervous system, the damaging noise levels, and ototoxic drugs. The remaining causes and risk factors of hearing loss in neonates are congenital infections and hyperbilirubinemia. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Perilymphatic Fistula -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Definitively identifying a labyrinthine fistula can be challenging, as no international consensus regarding diagnosis has been established. However, diagnostic criteria has been suggested by the Intractable Hearing Loss Research Committee of the Japanese Ministry of Health and Welfare, which are based on the presence of hearing impairment, aural fullness, tinnitus and disequilibrium in the context of middle/inner ear pathology or barotrauma, with or without visualization of a fistula via tympanotomy or the presence of cochlin-tomoprotein in the tympanic fluid. [16] Patients with suggestive symptoms and history only are considered to have a probable PLF, while additional confirmation via tympanotomy or laboratory studies constitutes a definite PLF.

retrieved_docs.title: Hearing Loss Assessment in Children -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In 1993, the National Institutes of Health published recommendations that all newborns obtain routine hearing screening by 3 months of age. The Joint Committee on Infant Hearing published its first position statement in 1994, with updates made in 2000, 2007, and most recently in 2019. The 2007 guidelines called for newborn hearing screening to be performed in all newborns by 1 month of age, with definitive diagnostic audiometric testing completed by 3 months for those who did not pass, and finally, with the initiation of an appropriate intervention by 6 months of age. [7] These recommendations have been adopted widely in the United States, with recent data indicating that 98% of all newborns completed initial screening for hearing loss within 1 month of birth. This led to the updated recommendation 2019 that screening, diagnostic testing, and intervention be completed in 1 month, 2 months, and 3 months, respectively, wherever possible.

retrieved_docs.title: Newborn Hearing Screening -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hearing impairment can cause a massive impact on child development, either on its language or speech development, poor academic performance, personal-social maladjustments, and emotional disturbances. [1] Prompt detection and appropriate intervention within the first six months of age has been demonstrated to decrease its adverse complications and improve language acquisition. Hence, universal newborn hearing screening has been extensively and strongly promoted and advocated as an early detection strategy for hearing loss in children.

retrieved_docs.title: Sensorineural Hearing Loss -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of sensorineural hearing loss varies in different countries. In the United States, sudden SNHL affects between 5-27 per 100,000 people each year, with approximately 66,000 new annual cases. [6] Due to different studies using varying thresholds when classifying hearing loss, there is little consensus in the literature regarding the epidemiology of age-related hearing known as presbycusis. In presbycusis, hearing loss prevalence doubles every decade of life from the second through to the seventh decade, and is nearly universal past the eighth decade of life. [7] Another important cause of hearing loss in the adult population is noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL). It has been estimated that 16% of adults worldwide disabling hearing loss is occupational noise related. [8] This remains a common occupational disease despite legislation in place in most developed countries to prevent NIHL.

retrieved_docs.title: Syndromic Sensorineural Hearing Loss -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Children with syndromic SNHL often present with a range of clinical symptoms and varying degrees of hearing impairment. Approximately 1/3 of genetic hearing loss is classified as syndromic. In some cases, the cause of the hearing loss is identifiable, while in others, extensive investigation and testing may be necessary to reach a diagnosis. Treatment may involve both medical and surgical interventions tailored to the specific needs of each patient. Regardless of the chosen approach, clinicians must demonstrate patience, compassion, and empathy, as a definitive cure is often not available. Patients with syndromic SNHL must adapt to their condition, and their care should focus on supporting their development and quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Newborn Hearing Screening -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Two recommended hearing screening techniques are the otoacoustic emissions (OAE) test, and the auditory brainstem evoked responses (ABRs) test. The OAE test is the most commonly performed test in newborn screening because of its easy, safe, and quick procedure. It is also inexpensive, easy to interpret, and provides a sensitive indication of the presence of hearing loss. It also does not require highly specialized manpower. It is particularly easy to carry out in this age group as their level of restlessness is lower than the older infants, and thereby precludes the need for sedation. If the result leads to a positive hearing loss or the test's result does not pass; thus, the newborn has to do the ABR test before three months of age. The ABR test is an auditory evoked electrophysiologic response that highly correlates with hearing and has been used successfully and cost-effectively to screen newborns and to identify further the degree and type of hearing loss.

retrieved_docs.title: Newborn Hearing Screening -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Some risk factors for newborn hearing loss are the history of using mechanical ventilation for five days, ototoxic medications, premature birth, low birth weight, admission to neonatal intensive care unit for more than seven days, and low APGAR score. [3] Unfortunately, more than 50% of babies with hearing loss do not have the risk factors mentioned above. Therefore, neonatal hearing screening is recommended for all newborns to dampen the misdiagnoses.

retrieved_docs.title: Audiology Clinical Masking -- Indications -- Rule 2
retrieved_docs.content: When there is a difference between the non-masked bone conduction threshold and the air conduction threshold of either ear is greater than 10dB.

retrieved_docs.title: Otoscopy -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Otoscopy is an essential clinical tool used to evaluate and diagnose conditions and diseases of the external and middle ear. This procedure involves examining the structures of the ear, specifically the external auditory canal, tympanic membrane, and middle ear. Clinicians perform otoscopies during routine wellness check-ups and when assessing specific ear-related complaints. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Audiogram Interpretation -- Potential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Audiogram interpretation aims to determine the type and degree of a patient’s hearing loss. [10] The table below defines the categories of hearing loss based on severity:

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Hearing Loss -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric hearing loss is a broad category that covers a wide range of pathologies. Early detection and prompt management are essential for the development of normal language and psychosocial functioning, as well as to identify potentially reversible causes or other underlying problems.  Hearing is measured in decibels, and the severity of the hearing loss is graded by hearing thresholds. The normal hearing range is 0-20 decibels (dB) which equates to being able to perceive sound quieter than a whisper. Mild hearing loss corresponds to a range of 20-39 dB, moderate 40-69 dB, severe 70-89 dB, and profound is greater than 90 dB.

retrieved_docs.title: Hearing Loss -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: An accurate hearing evaluation is possible for the population of all ages, though there are specific limitations that are age- or cognition-related regarding formal audiometric evaluation that may complicate the picture. According to the American Academy of Pediatrics as well as the Joint Committee on Infant Hearing, all infants should undergo a hearing evaluation to rule out any hearing impairment at birth or by the age of one month. [17] Additionally, all newborns and infants with hearing loss need to get a comprehensive evaluation that focuses on medical and birth history as well as a family history for the previous three generations, according to the American College of Medical Genetics and Genomics. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Nonorganic Functional Hearing Loss -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Normal hearing involves the complex, coordinated interplay of many closely associated anatomical structures within the ear and beyond. On the other hand, hearing loss occurs when pathology results in audiometric hearing thresholds falling below 25 decibels hearing level (dB HL) - a scale based on normative hearing thresholds as opposed to decibels of sound pressure level, which reflect the actual amount of sound energy produced. Damage or pathology of any of the multiple components of the auditory system, i.e., tympanic membrane, middle ear ossicles, cochlea, nerves, neural pathways, nuclei, and central processing centers, can contribute to hearing loss. Amongst the myriad causes of hearing loss, a distinct yet often unrecognized entity is nonorganic functional hearing loss.

retrieved_docs.title: Hearing Loss -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: According to the American National Standards Institute, hearing loss ranking is as follows: Slight hearing loss: 16 to 25 dB Mild hearing loss: 26 to 40 dB Moderate hearing loss: 41 to 55 dB Severe hearing loss: 71 to 90 dB Profound: over 90 dB

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2766
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” is linked to the concept of “komplikace” and to the Arabic word “حَصاة,” indicating an association between this qualifier and a stone‑related complication. In addition, “Allowed qualifier” is connected to “conditions associated with dizziness or vertigo,” which are also classified as “komplikace,” underscoring its relevance to vestibular‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1516
retrieved_docs.content: OPHTHALMOLOGICAL AND OTOLOGICAL PREPARATIONS are classified as SENSORY ORGAN DRUGS, indicating that these medications are intended for the treatment of sensory organs such as the eyes and ears. As sensory organ drugs, they are used to manage conditions affecting vision and hearing, and their categorization helps guide appropriate therapeutic use and regulatory classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1904
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on ear are classified as a subcategory within the broader group of diagnostické techniky otologické.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2766
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is identified as a medical condition whose broader parent term is the Arabic phrase اِضطِراب الرِّئَة, indicating that this Arabic term encompasses the concept of respiratory distress. Additionally, respiratory distress syndrome is cross‑referenced with the Arabic designation متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة, suggesting that both Arabic terms are synonymous or closely related descriptors for the same syndrome. This linkage clarifies the terminology used in multilingual clinical contexts and aids in consistent identification and communication of the condition across language barriers.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 833
retrieved_docs.content: Ophthalmologic and otologic diagnosis and treatment is described as classifying itself, indicating that the domain of ophthalmologic and otologic diagnosis and treatment serves as its own categorical framework for organizing related diagnostic and therapeutic procedures within the same specialty.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1933
retrieved_docs.content: Otitis media and related conditions are located in the middle ear.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2355
retrieved_docs.content: Overige aandoeningen van uitwendig oor is classified as a subcategory of other ear and sense organ disorders, indicating that this group of conditions affecting the external ear falls within the broader category of ear and sense organ disorders. This hierarchical relationship helps clinicians organize diagnoses and tailor patient care for external‑ear pathologies within the context of overall ear and sensory organ health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2703
retrieved_docs.content: The entity “Allowed qualifier” designates a set of clinical situations that include conditions associated with dizziness or vertigo, for which rehabilitation (rehabilitace) is indicated. In the same context, “Allowed qualifier” also links rehabilitation (rehabilitace) to the condition referred to by the Arabic term “الاِختِنَاق,” indicating that therapeutic rehabilitation is applicable to that disorder as well. This highlights that rehabilitation is a recommended intervention for both vestibular‑related dizziness/vertigo and the condition described by “الاِختِنَاق.”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 5 -- Community 2958
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is connected to conditions associated with dizziness or vertigo and is also linked to the broader concept of komplikace. Within this framework of komplikace, specific complications such as انسداد الحالب (ureteral obstruction) and الالتصاق (adhesion) are included. This relationship highlights that the allowed qualifier pertains both to vestibular‑related symptoms and to particular pathological outcomes represented by komplikace, encompassing ureteral blockage and tissue adhesion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2731
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier serves as a permissible modifier within the classification system (klasifikace) for a diverse set of medical concepts and procedures. In this framework, arboviry is an allowed qualifier that can be applied to klasifikace, indicating that arboviral infections are categorized under this classification. Likewise, e codes: place of occurrence functions as an allowed qualifier for klasifikace, enabling the coding of the geographic location where a health event occurs. The allowed qualifier is also used to annotate klasifikace for specific clinical entities such as تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي (abnormal chromosome analysis), nutritional deficiencies, rheumatoid arthritis and related disease, tympanoplasty, and قلع الأسنان (tooth extraction). This systematic use of allowed qualifiers ensures consistent and precise coding across various conditions and interventions, supporting accurate documentation, data analysis, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 262
retrieved_docs.content: treatments. Finally, the category “unspecified elective surgery for purposes other than remedying health states” is connected to other therapeutic ear procedures, indicating that certain elective ear surgeries are grouped together even when not performed to treat disease. Together, these interrelated classifications and procedural sites provide a comprehensive view of how ear, skin, subcutaneous, ocular, and personal‑history concepts are organized within medical coding and clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 539
retrieved_docs.content: of ear and sensory organ conditions. The condition acute and chronic tonsillitis is further classified under the parent term التهاب لوزي, aligning it with the general concept of tonsillitis. This hierarchical organization clarifies the relationships among specific diseases, broader disease groups, and the overarching classification system, facilitating consistent coding, epidemiological tracking, and clinical communication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 100
retrieved_docs.content: ear (střední ucho) with otitis media and with بضع طبلة الأذن, illustrating integration of diagnostic and therapeutic pathways. All of these entities are organized under the overarching concept of “Operations by approach,” which classifies each as a form of surgery, thereby providing a coherent hierarchy that links general therapeutic categories to specific surgical and diagnostic interventions, ties each procedure to its anatomical target, and associates those targets with relevant disease entities for precise medical documentation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1480
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is used to qualify the concept kompli­cace. This qualifier can be applied to several specific medical complications, including حَصاة (stone), قرحات الطرفين السفليين (lower‑limb ulcers) and اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy). In addition, kompli­cace is linked to conditions associated with dizziness or vertigo. Diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال عصبي سكري) is clinically associated with lower‑limb ulcers (قرحات الطرفين السفليين), reflecting the common clinical observation that neuropathic damage in diabetes predisposes patients to ulcer formation on the feet. Together, these relationships illustrate how the allowed qualifier describes various complications and how diabetic neuropathy and lower‑limb ulcers are interrelated in patient care.
